summary:
Robert Boyle (1627–1691 AD) was the Irish-born natural philosopher and chemist who helped transform chemistry from alchemy into a scientific discipline, establishing experimental method and quantitative observation as its foundations. Boyle's most famous contribution is Boyle's Law (1662) — the demonstration that at constant temperature, the pressure and volume of a gas are inversely proportional (PV = constant) — derived from systematic experiments with his air pump (built with the assistance of Robert Hooke) that became the foundation of pneumatics. The air pump itself was a major instrument of the [[event:104|Scientific Revolution]], enabling demonstrations of the vacuum that challenged Aristotelian 'nature abhors a vacuum' doctrine.

Boyle's The Sceptical Chymist (1661) was equally important: it attacked the alchemical tradition of the four elements (earth, air, fire, water) and the Paracelsian three principles (salt, sulphur, mercury), arguing that neither provided an adequate foundation for understanding matter. He proposed instead that matter was composed of small 'corpuscles' (particles) — a proto-atomic theory that anticipated Dalton's atomic theory by 150 years. Boyle also insisted that chemistry must be based on careful experiment and quantitative observation rather than theory, and that chemical knowledge should be public and shared rather than secret and proprietary — a methodological stance that aligned with the emerging culture of the Royal Society (which he helped found). His definition of an element as a substance that cannot be broken down further by chemical means directly influenced [[event:2461|Lavoisier's]] later work on chemical elements.
